stem:
You are the paediatric registrar in a regional emergency department reviewing a 5-week-old boy brought in by his mother with 4 days of paroxysmal cough now associated with brief blue-around-the-lips episodes during feeds. He was born at term by spontaneous vaginal delivery; mother received her routine antenatal whooping cough booster at 18 weeks rather than the recommended 20 to 32 weeks window. He has not yet received any of his own scheduled vaccinations.

On examination between paroxysms he is alert and afebrile. During an observed paroxysm he goes briefly cyanotic with SpO2 dropping to 87% before recovering. Chest auscultation between episodes is clear. There are no inspiratory crepitations and no hepatosplenomegaly. White cell count is 28 x10^9/L with a lymphocyte predominance and a nasopharyngeal Bordetella pertussis PCR is positive.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 158/min, BP 88/54 mmHg, RR 44/min, SpO2 94% room air between paroxysms, temperature 36.9 °C
data_table.Examination: Alert between paroxysms; cyanotic during paroxysm with SpO2 87%; chest clear on auscultation; no hepatosplenomegaly
data_table.Investigations: WCC 28 x10^9/L (5-15) with lymphocyte predominance; nasopharyngeal Bordetella pertussis PCR positive; CXR no focal consolidation

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate management?

options.text: Admit for inpatient observation and commence oral azithromycin 10 mg/kg once daily for 5 days
options.rationale: Infants under 6 months with confirmed pertussis and observed apnoea or cyanosis require admission for cardiorespiratory monitoring; macrolide therapy reduces transmission and is recommended within 21 days of cough onset. (Source: RCH CPG: Pertussis; CDNA SoNG)
source_refs: CDNA Series of National Guidelines Pertussis

options.text: Treat only household contacts with azithromycin prophylaxis and discharge the infant home today
options.rationale: Contact prophylaxis is recommended but does not substitute for treating an unwell case; the index infant needs both admission and macrolide therapy. (Source: CDNA SoNG; Australian Immunisation Handbook)
source_refs: CDNA Series of National Guidelines Pertussis

options.text: Admit for nebulised salbutamol with chest physiotherapy and reassess oxygenation hourly
options.rationale: Salbutamol and chest physiotherapy do not modify pertussis paroxysms and may aggravate cough; macrolide and supportive care are the standard. (Source: RCH CPG: Pertussis)

options.text: Discharge home with safety net advice and arrange a paediatric clinic review within 48 hours
options.rationale: Observed cyanosis or apnoea in a confirmed pertussis case under 6 months is an admission indication because deterioration can be sudden and unpredictable. (Source: RCH CPG: Pertussis)

options.text: Admit and commence intravenous benzylpenicillin 60 mg/kg six-hourly for presumed pneumonia
options.rationale: Benzylpenicillin has no activity against Bordetella pertussis and the chest is clear; targeted macrolide therapy is preferred over broad spectrum antibiotic cover. (Source: eTG Antibiotic; CDNA SoNG)
source_refs: CDNA Series of National Guidelines Pertussis

explanation.summary: Pertussis in infants under 6 months is the high-risk group, with apnoea, cyanosis, secondary pneumonia and rarely pulmonary hypertension as life-threatening complications. The maternal whooping cough booster is recommended at 20 to 32 weeks gestation in every pregnancy to provide passive immunity until the infant's own 6-week dose. Macrolides (azithromycin first-line) limit transmission and shorten infectivity; treatment is recommended within 21 days of cough onset though clinical benefit on symptom duration diminishes after the catarrhal phase. Household and close contact prophylaxis is offered to vulnerable contacts including infants and pregnant women.
key_points: Infants under 6 months with cyanosis or apnoea require admission
Azithromycin 10 mg/kg daily for 5 days is the AU paediatric standard
Maternal antenatal booster at 20 to 32 weeks is the key prevention
Lymphocytosis with WCC over 20 x10^9/L raises suspicion in classical picture
explanation.pearls: In infants under 12 months with pertussis, a peak white cell count above 50 x10^9/L predicts severe disease and pulmonary hypertension; exchange transfusion has been used in extreme cases per Royal Children's Hospital practice.

sources.label: CDNA Series of National Guidelines Pertussis

stem:
A 5-week-old first-born male is referred from a peripheral hospital with 7 days of forceful non-bilious vomiting after every feed. He has lost 280 g since his 4-week weigh-in. His mother reports he appears hungry immediately after vomiting and wants to feed again.

On examination he is sleepy, with sunken anterior fontanelle and dry mucous membranes. After a test feed visible peristalsis is observed crossing the upper abdomen left to right and a 2 cm olive-shaped mass is palpable just lateral to the right rectus border above the umbilicus. Capillary refill is 4 seconds. Venous blood gas shows pH 7.52, pCO2 52 mmHg, bicarbonate 36 mmol/L, chloride 88 mmol/L, potassium 3.0 mmol/L and an abdominal ultrasound has just confirmed pyloric muscle thickness 5 mm and pyloric channel length 19 mm.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 168/min, BP 78/48 mmHg, RR 36/min, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 36.8 °C
data_table.Examination: Sleepy, sunken fontanelle, dry mucous membranes; visible upper abdominal peristalsis; palpable olive; CRT 4 seconds
data_table.Investigations: pH 7.52, pCO2 52, HCO3 36, Cl 88, K 3.0; abdominal ultrasound pyloric muscle 5 mm, channel 19 mm

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Order an upper gastrointestinal contrast study before commencing any fluid resuscitation
options.rationale: Diagnosis is already confirmed on ultrasound; contrast studies add no information and delay correction of life-threatening electrolyte derangement. (Source: RCH CPG: Pyloric Stenosis)

options.text: Resuscitate with 20 mL/kg 0.9% saline bolus then maintenance with 0.9% saline and added potassium
options.rationale: The infant has hypochloraemic hypokalaemic metabolic alkalosis from gastric loss; pre-operative correction of chloride, potassium and bicarbonate with normal saline plus potassium is mandatory before pyloromyotomy. (Source: RCH CPG: Pyloric Stenosis; Royal Hospital for Women Neonatal Handbook)
source_refs: Royal Hospital for Women Neonatal Handbook

options.text: Trial of small volume thickened formula via nasogastric tube before any further intervention
options.rationale: Mechanical obstruction at the pylorus will not respond to thickened feeds; enteral feeds will be re-vomited and worsen dehydration. (Source: RCH CPG: Pyloric Stenosis)

options.text: Commence intravenous erythromycin 3 mg/kg six-hourly as a prokinetic before any surgery
options.rationale: Erythromycin is contraindicated in this age group due to risk of pyloric stenosis itself, and prokinetics cannot relieve mechanical obstruction. (Source: AMH)

options.text: Proceed directly to laparoscopic Ramstedt pyloromyotomy in the next available theatre
options.rationale: Pyloromyotomy is the definitive treatment but is NEVER an emergency; operating on a hypochloraemic hypokalaemic alkalotic infant risks postoperative apnoea from CSF alkalosis. (Source: RCH CPG: Pyloric Stenosis)

explanation.summary: Pyloric stenosis classically presents at 3 to 6 weeks of age with progressive non-bilious projectile vomiting in an otherwise hungry infant. Gastric loss of hydrogen and chloride produces a hypochloraemic hypokalaemic metabolic alkalosis. Ultrasound diagnostic criteria are muscle thickness over 3 mm and channel length over 14 mm. Preoperative correction of fluid deficit and electrolyte and acid-base derangement is essential because anaesthesia on an alkalotic infant predisposes to postoperative apnoea via reduced respiratory drive in CSF. Normal saline with potassium supplementation is given until chloride is over 100 mmol/L and bicarbonate is under 28 mmol/L. Definitive treatment is Ramstedt pyloromyotomy, usually laparoscopic.
key_points: Classic age 3 to 6 weeks, first-born male predominance
Hypochloraemic hypokalaemic metabolic alkalosis is the metabolic signature
US criteria: muscle thickness over 3 mm, channel length over 14 mm
Correct alkalosis before theatre to prevent postoperative apnoea
explanation.pearls: Pre-operative biochemical targets before Ramstedt pyloromyotomy: chloride over 100 mmol/L, bicarbonate under 28 mmol/L, potassium over 3.5 mmol/L per RCH guidance; surgery proceeds when these are met regardless of how many hours pass.

sources.label: Royal Hospital for Women Neonatal Handbook

stem:
A 16-month-old boy is brought to the general practice by his mother because preschool staff said he looks pale and is becoming a fussy eater. He was breastfed exclusively until 11 months and is now drinking around 1500 mL of cow's milk daily from a bottle. He refuses red meat and most vegetables but accepts crackers and yoghurt. Birth was uncomplicated and his immunisations are up to date.

On examination he is pale with no jaundice and no organomegaly. There is no lymphadenopathy and his developmental milestones are appropriate for age. There is no history of melaena and faecal occult blood testing is negative. Full blood count shows haemoglobin 78 g/L (105-135), mean corpuscular volume 58 fL (75-85), platelets 412 x10^9/L, white cell count 9.4 x10^9/L. Ferritin is 6 mcg/L (20-200), transferrin saturation 5%, CRP under 5.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 118/min, BP 92/55 mmHg, RR 26/min, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 36.8 °C
data_table.Examination: Pale, alert, no jaundice; no hepatosplenomegaly; no lymphadenopathy; developmental milestones appropriate
data_table.Investigations: Hb 78 g/L (105-135); MCV 58 fL (75-85); ferritin 6 mcg/L (20-200); transferrin saturation 5%; CRP under 5; FOBT negative

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line management?

options.text: Transfuse 10 mL/kg packed red cells before any oral iron supplementation is started
options.rationale: Transfusion is reserved for symptomatic anaemia with cardiorespiratory compromise; haemodynamically stable nutritional iron deficiency responds rapidly to oral iron. (Source: NBA Patient Blood Management)

options.text: Switch to a soy-based formula and arrange follow-up full blood count in 4 weeks
options.rationale: Soy formula does not correct established deficiency and the absorption of iron from soy formula is less than from cow's milk supplements; oral iron therapy is the primary treatment. (Source: RCH CPG: Iron Deficiency)

options.text: Commence oral elemental iron 3 mg/kg once daily and counsel to limit cow's milk to 500 mL
options.rationale: First-line for paediatric iron deficiency anaemia without active bleeding is oral elemental iron 3 to 6 mg/kg/day plus addressing dietary drivers; cow's milk over 500 mL/day inhibits iron absorption and is the primary modifiable factor here. (Source: RCH CPG: Iron Deficiency; AMH)

options.text: Trial a multivitamin without iron and cease cow's milk completely for 6 weeks
options.rationale: Multivitamins without iron will not correct iron deficiency, and complete milk cessation is unnecessarily aggressive; volume restriction is the appropriate intervention. (Source: RCH CPG: Iron Deficiency)

options.text: Refer to day stay for intravenous ferric carboxymaltose infusion as a single dose
options.rationale: IV iron is reserved for failure of oral iron, malabsorption or intolerance; oral iron is effective and cheaper as initial therapy with normal absorption. (Source: RCH CPG: Iron Deficiency)

explanation.summary: Iron deficiency in Australian toddlers most often follows prolonged exclusive milk feeding past 12 months, with intake over 500 mL/day inhibiting non-haem iron absorption and displacing iron-rich solids. Diagnostic biochemistry shows microcytic anaemia with low ferritin and low transferrin saturation; ferritin under 12 mcg/L is diagnostic in children. First-line treatment is oral elemental iron 3 to 6 mg/kg/day for 3 months to replenish stores, plus dietary counselling. Haemoglobin rise of around 10 g/L over 2 to 4 weeks confirms response. Failure to respond should prompt review of adherence, malabsorption (coeliac), or ongoing loss.
key_points: Cow's milk under 500 mL/day in toddlers, prefer iron-rich solids
Oral elemental iron 3 to 6 mg/kg/day for 3 months
Ferritin under 12 mcg/L is diagnostic in children
Persistent failure to respond, screen for coeliac disease
explanation.pearls: Oral iron is best absorbed on an empty stomach with vitamin C-containing fluid; co-administration with cow's milk, calcium or tea cuts absorption by approximately 50% per RCH CPG: Iron Deficiency.

stem:
You are the general paediatric registrar reviewing a 4-year-old previously well girl referred by her general practitioner with widespread petechiae on the trunk and lower limbs noticed by her mother yesterday. She had a coryzal illness ten days ago which resolved without treatment. There has been no epistaxis, gum bleeding, melaena or haematuria, and her mother denies any minor trauma that could explain the bruising. She is up to date with vaccinations and is fully immunised against measles.

On examination she is afebrile and well perfused, running around the consulting room. There is no lymphadenopathy, no hepatosplenomegaly and no bone tenderness. She has scattered petechiae on the trunk and a 1 cm wet purpura on the lower lip but no other mucosal bleeding. The full blood count shows haemoglobin 122 g/L, white cell count 7.4 x10^9/L with normal differential and morphology, and platelets 11 x10^9/L; the blood film comment from the haematologist is 'normal red cell and leucocyte morphology, occasional giant platelets, no blasts'.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 102/min, BP 96/58 mmHg, RR 22/min, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: Active, well perfused; scattered petechiae trunk and lower limbs; 1 cm wet purpura lower lip; no lymphadenopathy or hepatosplenomegaly
data_table.Investigations: Hb 122 g/L, WCC 7.4 x10^9/L with normal differential, platelets 11 x10^9/L; film: normal red cell and leucocyte morphology, occasional giant platelets, no blasts

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate management?

options.text: Admit for urgent bone marrow aspirate and trephine biopsy before any therapeutic decision
options.rationale: Bone marrow study is not required for the diagnosis of typical paediatric ITP when blood film is normal and there are no atypical features; pre-treatment marrow is only required if features suggest alternative pathology. (Source: RCH CPG: ITP)

options.text: Oral prednisolone 4 mg/kg/day for 4 days then taper over 14 days as outpatient
options.rationale: Short-course steroid is an alternative active treatment for paediatric ITP with significant bleeding but is not indicated for cutaneous-only bleeding because remission rate matches observation. (Source: RCH CPG: ITP)

options.text: Single dose intravenous immunoglobulin 1 g/kg as inpatient with observation overnight
options.rationale: IVIG is reserved for significant mucosal bleeding or before urgent procedures because it shortens time to platelet rise; it does not change long-term outcome and exposes the child to a blood product. (Source: RCH CPG: ITP)

options.text: Outpatient observation with written bleeding advice and repeat platelet count in 1 week
options.rationale: Australian guidance is to observe paediatric ITP with platelets above the bleeding threshold when there is only minor cutaneous bleeding; randomised data show no outcome benefit of immediate IVIG or steroid in the absence of significant bleeding. (Source: RCH CPG: ITP; HSANZ Position Statement)
source_refs: HSANZ Position Statement on Paediatric ITP

options.text: Single bag of platelets 10 mL/kg now and proceed to bone marrow examination today
options.rationale: Platelet transfusion is only indicated for life-threatening haemorrhage in ITP because transfused platelets are destroyed immediately by the same autoantibody. (Source: RCH CPG: ITP; NBA Patient Blood Management)

explanation.summary: Acute immune thrombocytopenia in children is the most common cause of new isolated thrombocytopenia, typically following a viral illness, with peak incidence between 2 and 6 years. The diagnosis is clinical, supported by isolated thrombocytopenia with normal white cell and red cell lines on a normal blood film. The treatment decision hinges on bleeding severity not platelet count; over 80% of paediatric cases resolve within 6 months and observation is preferred for cutaneous bleeding only. Active treatment options for significant bleeding are IVIG, short course steroid, or anti-D in Rh-positive children. Marrow examination is reserved for atypical features such as constitutional symptoms, lymphadenopathy, organomegaly, or abnormal blood film.
key_points: Watchful waiting is the AU first-line for paediatric ITP without significant bleeding
Marrow study only if blood film abnormal or atypical features
IVIG or steroid for active mucosal or life-threatening bleeding
Over 80% of paediatric ITP resolves spontaneously within 6 months
explanation.pearls: In paediatric ITP, the presence of wet purpura (mucosal bleeding) is a more useful trigger for treatment than the absolute platelet count, per RCH CPG; isolated cutaneous petechiae with platelets even under 10 x10^9/L can usually be observed safely.

sources.label: HSANZ Position Statement on Paediatric ITP

stem:
A 14-year-old girl is referred to the paediatric clinic from her general practitioner with three weeks of daily frontal headache that is worse on waking and worse on bending forward. She has also noticed transient grey-outs in both eyes lasting seconds when she stands up quickly. Her body mass index has risen from 27 to 31 kg/m2 over the last 12 months. She has been taking isotretinoin 30 mg daily for severe acne for four months and the combined oral contraceptive pill for three months for cycle control.

On examination her blood pressure is normal and she is afebrile. Visual acuity is 6/6 each eye. Fundoscopy reveals bilateral grade 2 papilloedema. Cranial nerve examination is otherwise normal. Non contrast magnetic resonance imaging shows partial empty sella, posterior globe flattening, prominent perioptic cerebrospinal fluid spaces and no mass lesion or venous sinus thrombosis. Lumbar puncture in the lateral decubitus position shows an opening pressure of 32 cmH2O with normal CSF cell count, glucose and protein.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 78/min, BP 118/72 mmHg, RR 16/min, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: Visual acuity 6/6 bilaterally; bilateral grade 2 papilloedema on fundoscopy; cranial nerves otherwise intact
data_table.Investigations: MRI brain: partial empty sella, posterior globe flattening, no mass, no venous sinus thrombosis; LP opening pressure 32 cmH2O, CSF cell count, glucose, protein within normal limits
data_table.Medications: Isotretinoin 30 mg daily for 4 months; combined oral contraceptive pill for 3 months

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial management?

options.text: High dose intravenous methylprednisolone 30 mg/kg pulse for 3 days then taper
options.rationale: Systemic steroids are no longer recommended in IIH because the rebound when ceased can be severe and they cause weight gain which worsens the disease. (Source: RCH CPG: IIH)

options.text: Refer for ventriculoperitoneal shunt insertion within 2 weeks for cerebrospinal fluid diversion
options.rationale: CSF shunting is reserved for refractory IIH with progressive vision loss despite maximal medical therapy; it is not first-line. (Source: RCH CPG: IIH)

options.text: Refer urgently for optic nerve sheath fenestration to protect visual function
options.rationale: Optic nerve sheath fenestration is reserved for fulminant visual loss despite medical therapy; visual acuity is preserved here so medical therapy should be tried first. (Source: RCH CPG: IIH; RANZCO Position Statement)

options.text: Sumatriptan 50 mg orally during attacks with propranolol 40 mg twice daily prophylaxis
options.rationale: Migraine pharmacotherapy does not address raised intracranial pressure and risks delaying treatment that could prevent permanent visual loss. (Source: National Headache Network Guidelines)

options.text: Start oral acetazolamide 25 mg/kg/day in divided doses and discontinue isotretinoin
options.rationale: First-line for paediatric IIH is acetazolamide plus identification and removal of the precipitant; isotretinoin is a recognised cause of secondary intracranial hypertension and must be stopped. (Source: RCH CPG: IIH; AMH Acetazolamide)

explanation.summary: Idiopathic intracranial hypertension in adolescents typically affects post-pubertal girls with rapid weight gain. Diagnosis requires papilloedema, normal MRI brain and MR venogram, and elevated CSF opening pressure (over 25 cmH2O in non-obese, over 28 cmH2O in obese adolescents) with normal CSF composition. Common secondary precipitants include isotretinoin, tetracyclines, vitamin A excess, growth hormone therapy and oral contraceptives; identification and cessation is essential. Acetazolamide is first-line; topiramate is an alternative when migraine coexists. Untreated IIH can cause permanent visual field loss from chronic papilloedema. Weight loss is a key non-pharmacological intervention.
key_points: Diagnosis: papilloedema, normal imaging, raised CSF pressure with normal composition
Secondary causes: isotretinoin, tetracyclines, vitamin A, growth hormone, COCP
First-line: acetazolamide plus precipitant removal plus weight loss
Surgical intervention for progressive visual loss despite medical therapy
explanation.pearls: Isotretinoin is the strongest medication-associated cause of secondary intracranial hypertension in adolescent acne treatment; the Roche Australian product information advises ceasing the drug at the first sign of headache and papilloedema.

stem:
A 3-year-old girl with known homozygous sickle cell disease is brought to the emergency department by her parents, who recently migrated from West Africa, with 6 hours of progressive lethargy and a visibly distended abdomen. Her baseline haemoglobin is 80 g/L. She received penicillin V prophylaxis from diagnosis at 9 months and is fully immunised including pneumococcal and meningococcal vaccines.

On examination she is pale and drowsy but responsive to her name. Capillary refill is 4 seconds and her hands are cool to the wrists. The abdomen is distended and the spleen is palpable extending to the umbilicus. There is no focal abdominal tenderness, no jaundice, and the chest is clear on auscultation with no murmur. Haemoglobin is 38 g/L, reticulocytes 12% (baseline 8%), white cell count 16 x10^9/L, platelets 124 x10^9/L. Group and crossmatch is in progress.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 162/min, BP 78/40 mmHg, RR 36/min, SpO2 98% room air, temperature 37.4 °C
data_table.Examination: Pale, drowsy; CRT 4 seconds; abdomen distended with spleen palpable to umbilicus; chest clear; no jaundice
data_table.Investigations: Hb 38 g/L (baseline 80); reticulocytes 12% (baseline 8%); WCC 16 x10^9/L; platelets 124 x10^9/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Urgent simple top-up transfusion of leucodepleted phenotyped red cells to a target haemoglobin
options.rationale: Acute splenic sequestration crisis with hypovolaemia is treated with urgent simple top-up transfusion of leucodepleted phenotyped red cells aiming to restore circulating volume without over-transfusing because the sequestered red cells will re-enter circulation. (Source: HSANZ Paediatric Haematology Handbook; Royal Children's Hospital Sickle Cell Pathway)
source_refs: Royal Children's Hospital Sickle Cell Pathway

options.text: Continue penicillin prophylaxis only and observe overnight with serial haemoglobin checks
options.rationale: Observation alone in haemodynamic decompensation risks death from hypovolaemic shock; transfusion is the time-critical intervention. (Source: HSANZ Paediatric Haematology Handbook)

options.text: Refer urgently for emergency splenectomy to remove the sequestering reservoir
options.rationale: Emergency splenectomy is not first-line in acute sequestration; the volume sequestered returns to circulation after transfusion and acute splenectomy is reserved for failed conservative management. (Source: HSANZ Paediatric Haematology Handbook)

options.text: Commence hydroxyurea 15 mg/kg/day and oxygen at 6 L/min via non rebreather mask
options.rationale: Hydroxyurea is an outpatient disease-modifying therapy and has no role in the acute crisis; oxygen alone does not address acute anaemic hypovolaemia. (Source: HSANZ Paediatric Haematology Handbook)

options.text: Urgent exchange transfusion targeting haemoglobin S fraction under 30% and oxygen via mask
options.rationale: Exchange transfusion is reserved for acute chest syndrome, stroke, or multi-organ failure; it is not first-line in isolated sequestration where volume restoration is the urgent need. (Source: HSANZ Paediatric Haematology Handbook)

explanation.summary: Acute splenic sequestration crisis is a life-threatening complication of sickle cell disease in children under 6 years, in whom the spleen has not yet auto-infarcted. Massive trapping of sickled red cells in the spleen produces sudden anaemia and hypovolaemic shock. The classical findings are a rapidly enlarging tender spleen, pallor, tachycardia, hypotension, and haemoglobin drop of at least 20 g/L below baseline. Management is urgent simple transfusion with leucodepleted phenotyped red cells, with caution to avoid over-transfusion because release of sequestered red cells can produce hyperviscosity. Recurrence rate is over 50% and elective splenectomy is offered after the second episode.
key_points: Sequestration: age under 6, enlarging spleen, anaemia and shock
Urgent simple transfusion to restore volume
Avoid over-transfusion: sequestered cells release on recovery
Recurrence over 50%; elective splenectomy after 2nd event
explanation.pearls: In acute splenic sequestration the transfusion target is typically half the haemoglobin deficit because sequestered red cells will re-enter circulation as the spleen recovers; aiming for full pre-crisis haemoglobin risks hyperviscosity per HSANZ paediatric guidance.

sources.label: Royal Children's Hospital Sickle Cell Pathway

stem:
A 12-day-old female infant is brought to the emergency department by her parents with poor feeding and three episodes of forceful vomiting today. She is the first child of non-consanguineous parents and was discharged at day 3 with a normal newborn screen pending. Birth weight was 3.4 kg and she has now lost 12% of her birth weight.

On examination she is mottled, lethargic and minimally responsive to handling. Her anterior fontanelle is sunken and capillary refill is 5 seconds. There is unexpected mild clitoromegaly and posterior labial fusion noted today by the registrar; the clitoral length is at the upper limit of normal and the urethral opening sits within the clitoral shaft. There is no abdominal mass and the chest is clear. Capillary blood gas shows pH 7.22, base excess minus 12. Bedside sodium is 122 mmol/L, potassium 7.4 mmol/L, glucose 2.6 mmol/L and the on-call biochemist has rung to confirm the newborn screening 17-hydroxyprogesterone is markedly elevated.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 184/min, BP 58/32 mmHg, RR 64/min, SpO2 98% room air, temperature 36.2 °C
data_table.Examination: Mottled, lethargic; sunken fontanelle, CRT 5 seconds; mild clitoromegaly and posterior labial fusion
data_table.Investigations: pH 7.22, base excess -12; sodium 122 mmol/L; potassium 7.4 mmol/L; glucose 2.6 mmol/L; 17-hydroxyprogesterone markedly elevated

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Intravenous insulin 0.05 units/kg/hour with dextrose infusion and urgent karyotype analysis
options.rationale: Insulin-dextrose is a temporising hyperkalaemia measure but the priority is reversing adrenal insufficiency with hydrocortisone; karyotyping is appropriate later but not now. (Source: RCH CPG: Adrenal Insufficiency)

options.text: Intravenous hydrocortisone 25 mg stat plus 0.9% saline 20 mL/kg bolus with 10% dextrose maintenance
options.rationale: Salt-losing CAH crisis is treated with immediate IV hydrocortisone 25 mg (under 6 months) plus 0.9% saline volume resuscitation and dextrose for hypoglycaemia; this corrects mineralocorticoid and glucocorticoid deficiency simultaneously. (Source: RCH CPG: Adrenal Insufficiency; Australasian Paediatric Endocrine Group)

options.text: Empirical intravenous cefotaxime 50 mg/kg and urgent lumbar puncture for septic workup
options.rationale: Sepsis workup may be appropriate alongside CAH treatment but does not address the dominant biochemical signature of hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia and hypoglycaemia in a virilised female. (Source: RCH CPG: Adrenal Insufficiency; RCH CPG: Sepsis)

options.text: Salbutamol 0.5 mL of 5 mg/mL nebulised solution with calcium gluconate 10% 0.5 mL/kg
options.rationale: Treating the hyperkalaemia symptomatically without addressing the underlying adrenal insufficiency is incomplete; hydrocortisone reverses the mineralocorticoid deficit causing the potassium rise. (Source: RCH CPG: Adrenal Insufficiency)

options.text: Intravenous 10% dextrose 5 mL/kg bolus then oral fludrocortisone 100 mcg via nasogastric tube
options.rationale: Oral fludrocortisone has no immediate effect and cannot be reliably absorbed in a shocked infant; intravenous hydrocortisone and saline are the time-critical interventions. (Source: RCH CPG: Adrenal Insufficiency)

explanation.summary: Congenital adrenal hyperplasia from 21-hydroxylase deficiency presents in the salt-losing form between days 7 and 21 of life, after maternal cortisol has waned. Affected female infants are virilised at birth (clitoromegaly, labial fusion) but normal external genitalia in males may delay recognition. The biochemical signature is hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia, metabolic acidosis, hypoglycaemia and grossly elevated 17-hydroxyprogesterone. Acute management is intravenous hydrocortisone 25 mg (under 6 months), 50 mg (6 months to 6 years) or 100 mg (over 6 years), with saline volume resuscitation and dextrose. Long-term management is glucocorticoid replacement (hydrocortisone) plus mineralocorticoid (fludrocortisone) plus salt supplementation.
key_points: Salt-losing CAH presents day 7 to 21 with vomiting, dehydration, shock
Biochemistry: low Na, high K, acidosis, hypoglycaemia, raised 17-OHP
IV hydrocortisone 25 mg (under 6 months) plus saline plus dextrose
Long-term: hydrocortisone plus fludrocortisone plus salt
explanation.pearls: Newborn screening for 17-hydroxyprogesterone has been included in the Australian programme since 2018 across all states; phone notification of a markedly elevated result must trigger immediate hydrocortisone and saline rather than awaiting confirmatory testing.

stem:
You are the on-call neonatology registrar at a tertiary centre called to a 72-hour-old term male infant on the postnatal ward. He fed well for the first 36 hours and was discharge planned. Over the last 12 hours he has become progressively lethargic, has refused 3 consecutive feeds, vomited twice and now has irregular hyperventilation. He is the second child of consanguineous parents; the first child died at 5 days of age of presumed sepsis without a confirmed cause.

On examination he is encephalopathic with poor tone and absent suck. Heart rate is regular and the chest is clear; the abdomen is soft with no organomegaly. There is no rash and the anterior fontanelle is soft. Capillary glucose is 4.2 mmol/L. Venous blood gas shows pH 7.50, pCO2 22 mmHg, bicarbonate 18 mmol/L with a normal anion gap. Sodium 138, potassium 4.0, lactate 2.0. Plasma ammonia returned 30 minutes ago at 580 micromol/L (under 50). LFTs and coagulation are within normal limits. Urinary ketones are negative.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 156/min, BP 64/38 mmHg, RR 76/min irregular, SpO2 98% room air, temperature 36.8 °C
data_table.Examination: Encephalopathic with poor tone, absent suck; soft abdomen, no organomegaly; soft fontanelle
data_table.Investigations: pH 7.50, pCO2 22, HCO3 18, normal anion gap; glucose 4.2 mmol/L; lactate 2.0; ammonia 580 micromol/L (<50); LFTs and coagulation within normal limits; urinary ketones negative

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Therapeutic hyperventilation with intubation and intravenous mannitol 0.5 g/kg for cerebral oedema
options.rationale: Cerebral oedema management is supportive but does not remove ammonia; only dialysis combined with protein withdrawal and dextrose suppression of catabolism reverses the metabolic insult. (Source: APEG Metabolic Emergencies)

options.text: Commence empirical intravenous cefotaxime and benzylpenicillin and obtain blood cultures
options.rationale: Sepsis is in the differential but the biochemical picture (normal glucose, low lactate, low CO2, high ammonia, no acidosis) is not septic; antibiotics may run concurrently but the priority is ammonia removal. (Source: RCH CPG: Sepsis)

options.text: Cease all protein intake, commence intravenous 10% dextrose and arrange urgent haemodialysis transfer
options.rationale: Hyperammonaemic encephalopathy with ammonia above 500 micromol/L and respiratory alkalosis in a neonate is a urea cycle disorder until proven otherwise; immediate protein withdrawal, high glucose to suppress catabolism and urgent dialysis (extracorporeal) are time critical because mortality and neurological injury rise sharply after 24 hours above 200 micromol/L. (Source: Royal Hospital for Women Neonatal Handbook; APEG Metabolic Emergencies)
source_refs: Royal Hospital for Women Neonatal Handbook

options.text: Loading dose of phenobarbitone 20 mg/kg for presumed neonatal seizures with maintenance therapy
options.rationale: There is no clinical seizure description and phenobarbitone treats the symptom not the cause; in urea cycle disorders, valproate is also contraindicated as it precipitates hyperammonaemia. (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal Seizures; AMH)

options.text: Intravenous thiamine, pyridoxine and biotin trial alongside continued enteral feeds at half rate
options.rationale: Vitamin cofactor trials are appropriate in suspected organic acidaemias with acidosis and ketosis; continuing protein feeds will worsen ammonia and risks fatal cerebral oedema. (Source: APEG Metabolic Emergencies)

explanation.summary: Urea cycle disorders are the leading cause of neonatal hyperammonaemia outside organic acidaemias and present typically between day 2 and day 5 of life after the catabolic load of feeding. The biochemical signature is a high ammonia with a primary respiratory alkalosis (ammonia stimulates respiratory drive), normal anion gap, normal glucose, normal lactate, and absent ketonuria. Organic acidaemias by contrast usually have metabolic acidosis, ketonuria, raised anion gap and lactate. Time to dialysis is the strongest determinant of neurological outcome. Acute management is the triad of protein withdrawal, 10% dextrose to suppress catabolism, and ammonia scavenger therapy (sodium benzoate plus sodium phenylacetate) en route to extracorporeal removal. A family history of unexplained neonatal death in a sibling is a critical clue.
key_points: Day 2 to 5 lethargy plus hyperventilation in a feeding term infant - check ammonia
Respiratory alkalosis with normal glucose, lactate, ketones suggests urea cycle disorder
Triad: stop protein, 10% dextrose, ammonia scavengers plus haemodialysis
Family history of unexplained neonatal death is a red flag
explanation.pearls: Plasma ammonia above 500 micromol/L in a neonate is a time-critical metabolic emergency that requires extracorporeal removal (haemodialysis or continuous renal replacement) within 4 hours per APEG; ammonia under 200 micromol/L for under 6 hours predicts intact long-term cognition.

sources.label: Royal Hospital for Women Neonatal Handbook

stem:
A 32-year-old woman attends your general practice clinic with a 12-month history of progressively heavier periods. She reports cycles every 28 days lasting 8 days, soaks through a super tampon and a pad together every 1 to 2 hours on day 2 and 3, and has passed coin-sized clots. She is increasingly tired but works full time. She and her partner plan to conceive in around 12 months and want to avoid hormonal contraception. There is no dyspareunia, no intermenstrual or post coital bleeding, and no family history of bleeding disorder.

On examination she is comfortable; abdomen is soft and non tender and no pelvic mass is palpable. Speculum and bimanual examination are normal. Cervical screening (HPV-based) is current and negative. Pelvic ultrasound is reported as a normal sized anteverted uterus with regular endometrial echotexture, no submucosal fibroid, no adenomyosis features and normal ovaries. Full blood count shows haemoglobin 104 g/L (115-160), MCV 72 fL (80-95), ferritin 8 mcg/L (30-150). Coagulation profile and von Willebrand panel are within normal limits.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 76/min, BP 112/68 mmHg, RR 16/min, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: Abdomen soft, non tender, no pelvic mass; speculum and bimanual normal; cervical screening current negative
data_table.Investigations: Pelvic US: normal uterus, no fibroid, no adenomyosis, normal ovaries; Hb 104 g/L, MCV 72 fL, ferritin 8 mcg/L; coagulation and von Willebrand panel within normal limits

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line pharmacological treatment?

options.text: Combined oral contraceptive pill containing ethinylestradiol 30 mcg and levonorgestrel 150 mcg
options.rationale: COCP reduces bleeding but is hormonal and contraceptive, which the patient has declined; choosing it ignores her stated preference. (Source: RANZCOG Heavy Menstrual Bleeding)

options.text: Endometrial ablation under outpatient hysteroscopy after diagnostic curettage at the same sitting
options.rationale: Endometrial ablation is a permanent solution that precludes future pregnancy and is contraindicated in a woman planning to conceive. (Source: RANZCOG Heavy Menstrual Bleeding)

options.text: GnRH agonist depot injection monthly for 6 months with low dose oestrogen add-back
options.rationale: GnRH agonists are reserved for short-term use before surgery and are not first-line for uncomplicated ovulatory heavy menstrual bleeding. (Source: RANZCOG Heavy Menstrual Bleeding)

options.text: Tranexamic acid 1 g orally three times daily for the first 4 days of each menstrual cycle
options.rationale: Tranexamic acid reduces menstrual blood loss by approximately 40 to 60% and is non hormonal; it is the recommended first-line for ovulatory heavy menstrual bleeding in a woman who has declined hormonal therapy and wishes to conceive. (Source: RANZCOG Heavy Menstrual Bleeding; AMH)

options.text: Levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine system inserted at the next available appointment
options.rationale: The Mirena IUS is highly effective for heavy menstrual bleeding but is a hormonal contraceptive that the patient has declined; first-line for women seeking conception within 12 months is non-hormonal tranexamic acid. (Source: RANZCOG Heavy Menstrual Bleeding)

explanation.summary: Heavy menstrual bleeding affects up to 25% of women and is defined as excessive bleeding interfering with quality of life. Initial workup includes FBC, ferritin, TSH, coagulation profile and von Willebrand panel if any bleeding history, plus pelvic ultrasound to exclude structural causes. RANZCOG and NICE first-line pharmacotherapy for ovulatory HMB without structural cause is tranexamic acid 1 g three times daily during menses for women not requiring contraception. The Mirena IUS is preferred for women happy with hormonal contraception. NSAIDs are an alternative for women with associated dysmenorrhoea. Iron supplementation runs concurrently and the patient here needs both oral iron and treatment of the bleeding.
key_points: Workup: FBC, ferritin, TSH, von Willebrand, pelvic ultrasound
First-line non hormonal: tranexamic acid 1 g three times daily during menses
First-line hormonal: Mirena LNG-IUS
Treat iron deficiency in parallel
explanation.pearls: Tranexamic acid is contraindicated only in active thromboembolism or history of arterial thrombosis; routine VTE risk does not preclude use per AMH and RANZCOG guidance.

stem:
A 28-year-old G1P1 woman is referred to your gynaecology clinic with 9 months of secondary amenorrhoea. Her cycle had previously been regular every 28 days. She had a normal vaginal delivery 14 months ago, complicated by retained products of conception that required ultrasound-guided suction curettage at 6 weeks postpartum after antibiotic treatment for endometritis. She has had cyclical pelvic pain in the lower abdomen each month for the last 6 months without any associated bleeding. She is not breastfeeding.

On examination she is healthy and well. Body mass index 24 kg/m2. Abdomen is soft. Speculum examination reveals a clean cervix; bimanual examination is unremarkable. Beta-hCG is negative. Serum TSH, prolactin, FSH, LH, oestradiol and androgen panel are within the normal range. A progesterone withdrawal challenge with medroxyprogesterone 10 mg daily for 10 days produced no bleeding. Transvaginal ultrasound shows a thin discontinuous endometrium with focal anechoic areas suggesting adhesions; a saline infusion sonohysterogram shows fibrous bands crossing the uterine cavity.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 72/min, BP 116/72 mmHg, RR 14/min, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: Healthy, BMI 24; abdomen soft; speculum and bimanual unremarkable
data_table.Investigations: Beta-hCG negative; TSH, prolactin, FSH, LH, oestradiol and androgens within normal limits; progesterone withdrawal: no bleed; TV US: thin discontinuous endometrium; saline sonohysterogram: intrauterine adhesions

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Diagnostic laparoscopy with chromopertubation to exclude tubal disease before any treatment
options.rationale: The diagnosis is intracavity not tubal; laparoscopy is not the next step and adds operative risk. (Source: RANZCOG Asherman Syndrome)

options.text: Repeat dilation and curettage to clear suspected retained tissue with antibiotic cover
options.rationale: Blind dilation and curettage created the adhesions and risks creating more; hysteroscopic adhesiolysis under direct vision is the correct procedure. (Source: RANZCOG Asherman Syndrome)

options.text: Empirical GnRH agonist therapy for 3 months and reassess endometrial pattern by ultrasound
options.rationale: GnRH agonists suppress ovulation and are used pre fibroid surgery; they do not lyse adhesions and worsen the endometrial thinning. (Source: RANZCOG Asherman Syndrome)

options.text: Combined oral contraceptive pill for 6 months and reassess menstrual return after cessation
options.rationale: COCP does not treat established adhesions; even high dose oestrogen alone cannot lyse fibrous bands and ongoing amenorrhoea will continue. (Source: RANZCOG Asherman Syndrome)

options.text: Hysteroscopic adhesiolysis under direct vision with post operative oestrogen for 8 weeks
options.rationale: Hysteroscopic adhesiolysis is the standard treatment for Asherman syndrome; postoperative oestrogen stimulates endometrial regeneration and reduces adhesion reformation. (Source: RANZCOG Asherman Syndrome)

explanation.summary: Asherman syndrome describes intrauterine adhesions that follow uterine instrumentation, especially curettage in the postpartum or post miscarriage state when endometrial vulnerability is highest. Patients present with hypomenorrhoea or amenorrhoea, cyclical pain from haematometra, and secondary infertility. The diagnostic gold standard is hysteroscopy but saline infusion sonohysterography is a reasonable non invasive screen. Treatment is hysteroscopic adhesiolysis with cold scissors or fine bipolar to preserve endometrium, followed by postoperative oestrogen and intracavity barriers (intrauterine balloon, hyaluronic acid gel) to reduce reformation. Severe disease may require staged procedures.
key_points: Cause: uterine instrumentation in vulnerable endometrium, e.g. postpartum
Presentation: amenorrhoea, cyclical pain, infertility
Diagnosis: hysteroscopy (gold standard); SIS as a screen
Treatment: hysteroscopic adhesiolysis plus post op oestrogen plus barrier
explanation.pearls: Postoperative oestrogen replacement (oestradiol valerate 2 mg twice daily for 6 to 8 weeks) is recommended after hysteroscopic adhesiolysis in Asherman syndrome to stimulate endometrial regrowth and reduce adhesion reformation per RANZCOG.
